role_summary: SNCC organizer 1963-1966; founding staff member of the SNCC Atlanta office and the Mississippi field projects; series associate producer of Eyes on the Prize (1987 and 1990, the Blackside Inc. documentary series that became the canonical filmed history of the civil rights movement); co-editor of Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (University of Illinois Press, 2010).
ai_reading: Richardson's top semantic neighbor is Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7) at a cosine count of 172; the broader cluster includes Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (entry 30), Reginald Robinson (entry 101), and Juadine Henderson (entry 68). The cluster is the SNCC-staff cohort (Atlanta office and Mississippi field) that Richardson worked alongside in 1963-1966. The catalog flags a +0.72 reading on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis (far toward Strategic Vision) as the most-distinctive structural reading. For a documentary-producer career across Eyes on the Prize and Blackside Inc, this is the catalog's clearest case of an embedding signature catching the long-form-narrative-construction register of her professional work over the tactical-on-the-ground register of her 1963-1966 SNCC organizing; the structure-vs-tactic distinction the axis names is itself one of the things Eyes on the Prize taught its viewers to see, and Richardson's testimony reads as the production-side voice rather than the field-side voice.
biographical_paragraph: While Richardson's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), her external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1]. Richardson grew up in Tarrytown, New York and enrolled at Swarthmore College in 1962. After participating in the 1963 Cambridge, Maryland desegregation campaign she suspended her studies and joined SNCC's Atlanta office full-time, working there and on Mississippi field projects across 1963-1966. After SNCC's late-1960s organizational decline she moved into documentary production at Henry Hampton's Blackside Inc in Boston, eventually serving as associate producer on the landmark Eyes on the Prize series (1987's six-hour Eyes on the Prize: America's Civil Rights Years, 1954-1965 and 1990's eight-hour Eyes on the Prize II: America at the Racial Crossroads, 1965-1985) [src: 2]. She subsequently produced or consulted on several PBS American Experience documentaries; she co-edited Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (with Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, who is herself catalog entry 83, Jean Smith Young, and others; University of Illinois Press, 2010), the canonical scholarly volume on women's organizing within SNCC [src: 2]. She has taught at Brown University as Distinguished Visiting Scholar in the Department of Africana Studies [src: 2].
movement_context: Richardson joined the SNCC Atlanta national office in 1963 as secretary to Executive Director James Forman, the role that put her at the operational center of SNCC's institutional life across the 1963-1966 period [src: 2]. The job description as the SNCC Digital Gateway records it (communication with pro-bono legal counsel, managing Forman's calendar and correspondence, covering the Wide Area Telephone Service line that the Atlanta office used to maintain real-time contact with the field-staff and the jailed-organizer network across Mississippi, Alabama, and southwest Georgia) placed her alongside Betty Garman Robinson (catalog entry 7), who arrived in March 1964 to coordinate the Friends of SNCC chapter network that financed the field operations [src: 3]. The two were neighbors on the same Atlanta office staffing chart through the 1964 Freedom Summer and the 1965 Selma campaign; the embedding picks up the proximity as the +172 cosine count between their oral histories that the AI's-reading observation names. The spring 1964 sit-in Richardson participated in (organized to protest that only women on the Atlanta staff were assigned to take meeting minutes, leading the men eventually to share that responsibility) is the SNCC Atlanta office's documented internal organizing moment over the sexual division of labor, the same internal-organizing tradition the 2010 collective memoir Hands on the Freedom Plow would document twenty-five years later [src: 2]. Richardson left SNCC in 1966, the year of the November Kingston Springs Tennessee staff meeting that elected Stokely Carmichael (catalog gallery image) SNCC chair and adopted the Black Power program [src: 4]. Her subsequent move to Henry Hampton's Blackside Inc in Boston placed her on the production team for Eyes on the Prize, the 14-episode PBS documentary series broadcast in two parts (1987's six-hour Eyes on the Prize: America's Civil Rights Years, 1954-1965 and 1990's eight-hour Eyes on the Prize II: America at the Racial Crossroads, 1965-1985), executive-produced by Hampton (1940-1998) under Blackside's American Experience contract [src: 5]. The series won Emmy Awards, a Peabody, and an International Documentary Award, and the historiographical scholarship since the late 1980s has treated it as the foundational filmed framing of the movement for a generation of American secondary-school and college audiences [src: 5]. The accompanying Juan Williams companion volume (1987) and the Henry Hampton and Steve Fayer oral-history-companion Voices of Freedom (1990) extended the documentary's reach into print bibliography [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: After Eyes on the Prize wrapped, Richardson continued her documentary production career at Northern Light Productions, the Boston-based media studio that has operated for more than four decades producing museum installations, interactive exhibits, and documentary films for cultural institutions [src: 7]. Her 2010 co-editorship of Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (University of Illinois Press) anchors the catalog cross-link to Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (catalog entry 30), Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (catalog entry 83), Betty Garman Robinson (catalog entry 7), and Jean Smith Young as her five co-editors; the volume collects fifty-two first-person accounts and stands as the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort [src: 8]. The 2014 paperback New Press reissue extended the volume's classroom adoption through the 2014 SNCC 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University in Raleigh that the SNCC alumni network convened to mark the organization's founding [src: 8]. Richardson serves on the board of the SNCC Legacy Project, the alumni organization that worked with Duke University to produce the SNCC Digital Gateway, the online documentary platform whose own biographical entry on Richardson the AI's reading and biographical paragraph above draw from [src: 2]. Her continuing teaching role at Brown University as a Distinguished Visiting Scholar in the Department of Africana Studies places her in active classroom contact with the next generation of researchers working in the institutional-history tradition the Eyes on the Prize series and Hands on the Freedom Plow together opened [src: 2].

gallery.alt: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) organizational logo, 1961
gallery.caption: SNCC organizational logo, 1961. Richardson joined the SNCC Atlanta national office in 1963 as secretary to Executive Director James Forman, working under this insignia through her departure in 1966. The vector mark is an SVG redrawing of a SNCC organizational pin first produced in 1961.
gallery.photographer: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (organizational mark; vector redrawn by Wikimedia contributor Gunnar.offel from the original SNCC pin)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: The Student Voice newsletter, March 26, 1965, front page
gallery.caption: The Student Voice, SNCC's principal in-house publication, March 26, 1965 front page. The issue covered the March 7, 1965 Bloody Sunday violence at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama, including the beating of SNCC chair John Lewis. Richardson was at her desk in the SNCC Atlanta national office through this period, working the WATS-line that connected the office to the Selma field-staff. Held by the Seattle Public Library and digitized through the Digital Public Library of America.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Seattle Public Library and DPLA)

gallery.alt: Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture) at a Mississippi press conference, 1966
gallery.caption: Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture, 1941-1998) at a Mississippi press conference in 1966. The 1966 SNCC year was Richardson's final year on the Atlanta national office staff; the May 1966 SNCC staff meeting at Kingston Springs, Tennessee elected Carmichael chair (succeeding John Lewis) and adopted the Black Power program that reorganized SNCC's institutional direction for the late 1960s. Richardson left SNCC in 1966 to return to college; her subsequent career at Henry Hampton's Blackside Inc and on Eyes on the Prize would later document this leadership transition for the 1990 Eyes on the Prize II series.
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)
